scenario: My asthma is not responding to my inhalers
patient_utterances: [Clinician asked: "Hi there. Good morning. I'm Doctor <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> from GP at Hand. Nice to see you."] Hi.
[Clinician asked: "OK. So, um, before we start your appointment. Could I confirm your full name and date of birth please?"] Yeah, it's John Smith, and I am thirty two years old.
[Clinician asked: "Sorry, I didn't catch your date of birth."] I'm thirty two years old.
[Clinician asked: "Thirty two years old, OK. Could you tell me the first line of your address, and your postcode please?"] It's four Park Avenue, and it's AB one CB two.
[Clinician asked: "That's great, thank you so much for that. Are you in a private place where you're OK to speak at the moment?"] Yes.
[Clinician asked: "OK. So how can I help you today? You've been having some problems with your asthma. Is that right?"] Sorry, say that .

[Clinician asked: "How can I help you today?"] So, um, I've been experiencing very weird symptoms, over the past month or so. And I don't know what's causing them. I have googled it and, um, nothing's coming up, so I thought I'd come and speak to you about it.
[Clinician asked: "OK. OK. So, tell me a bit more about these weird symptoms you've been having."] So, it started with, feeling very tired, but for a couple of days, and then I get back to normal.
[Clinician asked: "<INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>"] And then I started losing feeling, in my hands and arms. I get a weird sensation like, uh, I guess, pins and needles.
[Clinician asked: "Right <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> again, the connection's not so great. You've been getting pins and needles in the hand. Is that right?"] And. Yeah, that's correct.
[Clinician asked: "OK. Is that the right or left hand?"] My right hand.
[Clinician asked: "Is it always the right?"] Yes.

[Clinician asked: "OK. Um, we've lost video now, maybe is there a, a, a button you can press? OK. Are you happy for us to carry on without the video? I can't see you at the moment."] Yes, that's fine.

[Clinician asked: "All right. Um, so, you've been having these pins and needles, and episodes of numbness, in the right hand. Is it just the hand, or does it go up the arm as well?"] Uh, the pins and needles are in the hand, but I feel like my arm is getting weaker, but it only lasts few days and then goes back to normal. So, it's very weird.

[Clinician asked: "Right, OK. Um, is there any involvement of your leg at all?"] Uh, my, colleague said, I'm walking slightly differently to normal. And again, it's only for three days, or three or four days.
[Clinician asked: "OK. You, you said you've been having weird symptoms for about a month."] Yes.

[Clinician asked: ", so, what has been happening from the beginning of the month? What else has been happening?"] Um, I generally feel tired, and weak. And, sometimes I feel nauseous, and I don't feel like I want to do anything.
[Clinician asked: "Right, OK. Um, and, um. You know, , your colleague said you've, you've not been walking normally."] Yes.

[Clinician asked: "Do you feel anything different, on either side, in terms of your legs? Can , have you noticed anything different?"] Yeah, so, at the same time I get my arm symptoms, my leg feels weaker.
[Clinician asked: "Right, OK. And. You know you, the abnormal gait. Has that been present, outside of these episodes?"] No, it has not. So normally, I'm, fit and well actually.

[Clinician asked: "Right, . All right, OK. Um, and, these, these episodes. How often have they been happening?"] They have been getting more regular. So at the start, it would be maybe once a week, uh, to every once , once a month, but now it's getting uh, uh, a couple of times a day.
[Clinician asked: "Right, OK. And, how long have these episodes been going on for? When, when did it all start? Was it a month ago?"] , probably a month and a half, maybe two months.

[Clinician asked: "Right, OK. OK. And, along with these symptoms, of the weakness and numbness. Do you have any other symptoms at the same time? Like swallowing, or difficulty speaking, or vision problems?"] So, recently I've noticed, I can't hold my cup of coffee. And when I, do drink coffee, it's slightly harder to drink. And that's only been in the last couple of days, I guess.
[Clinician asked: "Right. Is that during these episodes, or outside of these episodes?"] Only during the episodes.
[Clinician asked: "OK. Do you have any difficulty swallowing at all?"] Not currently, no.
[Clinician asked: ". During the episodes, do you have any difficulty swallowing?"] I, I, I feel like there's something in my throat.
[Clinician asked: "OK. What about,  um, difficulty speaking during these episodes? Any, any issues with that at all?"] No, I can speak fine.
[Clinician asked: "OK. And any visual problems during these episodes?"] I, I don't think so, but I wear glasses.

[Clinician asked: "OK, all right. Now um, can I just ask? , has, uh, is there any history of stroke, or mini-strokes in your family?"] Uh, yes. My, father has had a, operation to the side of his neck, to reduce the risk of stroke.

[Clinician asked: "Right, OK. Um, and, uh, how about yourself? Have you had any history of, mini-strokes or anything like that, along those lines? I know you're quite young."] No, so after my, dad had his operation, I was required to be checked, and my cholesterol , was fine.

[Clinician asked: "Good, excellent. So you had a, a blood test done. And did they check for diabetes as well?"] Yeah. They checked my diabetes, my blood pressure and my, cholesterol, and the doctor said it was all OK.
[Clinician asked: "Fantastic, OK. Now, do you have any other illnesses at all?"] No.
[Clinician asked: "No. Are you on any medication?"] I take an aspirin, seventy five milligrams, once a day, but that's, because of something I read on the internet.
[Clinician asked: "OK. So, so, why is that? Why do you take that?"] I think it's, meant to reduce your risk of stroke and heart attack.

[Clinician asked: "OK. Do, have you ever told a doctor before, your GP, that you're on Aspirin?"] Yes I took, I started to , taking it after my, father had his operation, but that wasn't the doctor telling me.
[Clinician asked: "OK. Uh, and, has any GP ever spoken to you about, the risks of Aspirin, and whether you should be on it? Or have you not , told the , the GP in the past?"] No, I haven't.
[Clinician asked: "You haven't, OK. Um, all right. Um. Now, um, in terms of your uh, your kind of social situation. Um, are you working at the moment?"] Yes, I am.
[Clinician asked: ", what do you work as?"] Uh, I'm, uh, I'm actually a doctor.
[Clinician asked: "You're a , a, a medical doctor?"] Yes.
[Clinician asked: "OK. Uh, which, specialty are you in?"] I work in, uh, plastic surgery.
[Clinician asked: "Plastic surgery, OK, great. And, um, do you, have any, um, allergies to any medication at all?"] I'm allergic to penicillin only.
[Clinician asked: "OK. And, um, are you, um, living alone?"] No, I live with my wife.
[Clinician asked: "Right, OK. Um, fantastic. Uh , do you smoke at all?"] No, I don't.
[Clinician asked: "Right, OK. Um, that's fine. Now, obviously you're a, you're a medic yourself. Did you have any thoughts as to what might be going on?"] Uh, I've actually, got no idea.
[Clinician asked: "Right, OK. Um, well the, the way you're describing these episodes. They make me concerned about, mini-strokes or TIAs, , transient ischemic attacks. Um."] OK.

[Clinician asked: "And so, that's something quite urgent. And given that you've got a family history of um, stroke. Um, even though you're quite young, I think um, it's something that we need to exclude. So, what I'm probably going to do, uh, in fact what I am going to do, is I'm going to, refer you to a stroke, a TIA"] No, I'm perfectly fine at the moment.

[Clinician asked: "OK. So, so, what I'm gonna do, is I'm uh, I'm gonna refer you to a, a mini-stroke clinic, or a TIA clinic. Um, I send that referral off now, and then you can attend them, they, they'll usually, call you in within the next one to two days, OK. Because it's something relatively urgent. Now, today it's"] Sure.

[Clinician asked: "Now, if your symptoms return and they persist, they're prolonged. Then um, you may need to attend A and E. OK, because um, our presumptive, working diagnosis is, mini-stroke or TIA, OK. And if, a mini-stroke is prolonged then uh, then we end up thinking that this might be a stroke and in that case, "] Sure.

[Clinician asked: "Now if , things happen again, feel free to contact us, we can assess you over the phone, we can give you direction and guidance over the phone. That's, that's, that's not a problem, you know, you, you've got easy access to us. OK. So, um, this is the plan going forward. Um, I'm going to, um, do that"] OK.

[Clinician asked: "And um, if you want, you can ring them, either today or tomorrow morning. , today in the afternoon, wait uh, one or two hours. Or tomorrow morning just to find out when they got time to slot you in. And, just to make sure they've got you in the system, that they've got in the books, and they're goin"] <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>

[Clinician asked: "<INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>, this isn't, a, an immediate emergency that you need to rush down to A and E. But it's something quite urgent we need to, deal with in a short space of time, OK. So if there's, if there's any delay that, transpires for example, you can't make the appointment or, you know, they, y"] <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>
[Clinician asked: "All right. Did you have any questions at all?"] No, I think that covers, most of it.

[Clinician asked: "OK, great. So just bear with me a few minutes, and then I'll send that uh, all through and then you should, um, you should be able to help, you should have the information at your hands to, in order to take things further, OK."] , thank you.
[Clinician asked: "All the best. Take care now. Bye bye."] <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>
gold_note:
(the above consultation reason doesn't correlate with the patient's presentation)
Hx: episodes of unilateral right hand numbness/ pins and needles, with some right leg weakness and noticeable gait disturbances, ongoing for 1.5 months
occurring with increasing frequency- initially once a week, now more often, occurring daily
 symptoms not present now
no dysphagia, no dysphasia
no Pmhx of CVA/TIA
Father had CVA- appears to have had carotid endarterectomy
Non smoker, normal blood tests for diabetes/ cholesterol when checked a few years ago
PMHx and FH: otherwise nil of note
DH PT IS TAKING Aspirin daily on his own accord due to his FH
SH: pt is a medical doctor, plastic surgeon, lives with wife
Imp: TIAs- despite young age (need to exclude any systemic disease, eg antiphospholipid syndrome, that could be cause)
Plan: needs to be seen urgently in TIA clinic- will fax referral now. Pt to contact them via PALS/ clinic no to ensure f/u
If any prolonged symptoms occur- to attend A/E

gold_sections.PRESENTING_COMPLAINT: My asthma is not responding to my inhalers

naive.response_text:
Dear John,

I understand that you're experiencing some unsettling symptoms over the past month, including fatigue, weakness, nausea, and episodes of numbness and tingling in your right hand, along with a slight change in your gait. These symptoms are concerning, and I want to ensure you get the care you need.

Given your symptoms and family history of stroke, it's important to consider the possibility of transient ischemic attacks (TIAs), which are sometimes referred to as mini-strokes. While I understand you're a doctor, it's crucial to have these symptoms evaluated by specialists to rule out any serious conditions and to determine the best course of action.

I've referred you to a TIA clinic for further evaluation. They will likely conduct imaging and other tests to assess your situation. In the meantime, if you experience any prolonged episodes or if your symptoms worsen, please seek immediate medical attention at the emergency room.

Please don't hesitate to reach out if you have any questions or concerns. We're here to support you through this process.

Take care, and I look forward to hearing from you.

Best regards,  
[Your Name]  
GP at Hand

strong_prompt.response_text:
Thank you for sharing your concerns with me, John. It's clear you've been experiencing some unsettling symptoms, and I appreciate your proactive approach in seeking help. Given your background as a medical professional, I understand your concerns are heightened, especially with a family history of stroke.

It's completely reasonable to feel worried about these symptoms, particularly the pins and needles sensation in your right hand, weakness, and changes in your gait. These could be signs of a transient ischemic attack (TIA), often referred to as a mini-stroke, which is something we need to evaluate promptly. Research indicates that early assessment is crucial for such symptoms [PMID ?].

To move forward, I'll refer you to a TIA clinic for specialized evaluation. They should contact you within the next couple of days. In the meantime, if your symptoms worsen or persist, it's important to seek immediate care at the emergency room.

Before we conclude, could you summarize our plan for me? This will help ensure you're clear on the next steps. Remember, you can reach out if you have any questions or need further assistance. Take care, and I look forward to hearing from you soon.

It sounds like you're dealing with a lot of strange symptoms that are worrying you. You mentioned things like numbness in your hands and arms, weird sensations, fatigue, and nausea. I want to make sure I understand how all these symptoms are affecting your daily life.
